cinematic/009.title: File:MSH80 eruption mount st helens 05-18-80-dramatic-edit.jpg
cinematic/009.description: On May 18, 1980, at 8:32 a.m. Pacific Daylight Time, a magnitude 5.1 earthquake shook Mount St. Helens. The bulge and surrounding area slid away in a gigantic rockslide and debris avalanche, releasing pressure, and triggering a major pumice and ash eruption of the volcano. Thirteen-hundred feet (400 meters) of the peak collapsed or blew outwards. As a result, 24 square miles (62 square kilometers) of valley was filled by a debris avalanche, 250 square miles (650 square kilometers) of recreation, timber, and private lands were damaged by a lateral blast, and an estimated 200 million cubic yards (150 million cubic meters) of material was deposited directly by lahars (volcanic mudflows) into the river channels. Fifty-seven people were killed or are still missing. USGS Photograph taken on May 18, 1980, by Austin Post.
cinematic/009.creator: Austin Post
cinematic/009.credit: Huge tif converted to jpeg and caption from USGS Mount St. Helens, Washington May 18, 1980 Eruption Images
cinematic/009.title_signature: msh eruption mount st helens dramatic

cinematic/013.title: File:Snowfall at night over Brofjorden and Preemraff oil refinery.jpg
cinematic/013.description: View from Holländaröd looking over Brofjorden to Ryxö Island and Preemraff oil refinery behind it on a snowy night. On such nights, the snow flakes catch and reflects the light in a way that lights up the whole sky over the fjord. Holländaröd, Lysekil Municipality, Sweden. The big main glow comes from the product terminal where the tankers are loading. Two of the refinery's three orange flames are visible. The photo is taken about 5 km (3.1 mi) from the refinery trough falling snow that makes the distant features a bit fuzzy. The falling snow was moving away from this shore, leaving the jetty with the dock fingers relatively sharp.

cinematic/020.title: File:BLM Winter Bucket List -22- Eagletail Mountains Wilderness, Arizona, for Hikes with Dramatic Scenery (16296884782).jpg
cinematic/020.description: BLM Winter Bucket List #22: Eagletail Mountains Wilderness, Arizona, for Hikes with Dramatic Scenery The 97,880-acre Eagletail Mountains Wilderness is about 65 miles west of Phoenix, Arizona. The wilderness includes 15 miles of the Eagletail Mountains ridgeline to the north, Cemetary Ridge to the south, and a large desert plain area between the two ridgelines. The magnificent rocky spires and beauty of the Eagletail Mountains Wilderness can be explored along the Ben Avery Trail. Originally an old jeep route, the trail winds south into a wash and then to the bluffs next to Indian Spring. A climb up the hillside to the west reveals the dramatic scenery of the Eagletail Mountains, including two natural arches. The best seasons to hike the trail are fall, winter, and spring. Learn more about Eagletail Mountains Wilderness, a part of the BLM’s National Conservation Lands: www.blm.gov/az/st/en/prog/blm_special_areas/wildareas/eag... Photos by Bob Wick, BLM
cinematic/020.creator: Bureau of Land Management

cinematic/027.title: File:Hyatt Regency Embarcadero Atrium, San Francisco, US.jpg
cinematic/027.description: Nestled in the heart of San Francisco’s iconic waterfront district, California, US, the Hyatt Regency Embarcadero boasts one of the city’s most awe-inspiring architectural gems—the stunning 17-story atrium. Designed by renowned architect John Portman, this soaring interior was once the world’s largest hotel atrium when it opened in 1973, setting the stage for Portman’s revolutionary concept of communal hotel spaces. The vast, light-filled atrium welcomes guests with its open design, featuring exposed glass elevators, futuristic lighting, and sweeping walkways that guide your eyes upward to the dramatic skylights. The atrium’s symmetrical lines and angular design create an almost sci-fi ambiance, perfect for fans of modern architecture. This cutting-edge design has also made the Hyatt Regency a favorite filming location, most notably featured in the 1977 thriller High Anxiety by Mel Brooks. The atrium seamlessly blends with the waterfront energy outside, just steps from the Ferry Building, offering unbeatable views of the San Francisco Bay and the Bay Bridge. Its proximity to the Embarcadero makes it ideal for both business and leisure travelers seeking luxury with a touch of history. In addition to its architectural flair, the hotel offers high-end amenities like a 24-hour fitness center, fine dining, and spacious, modern rooms with floor-to-ceiling windows, all encapsulating the spirit of San Francisco’s vibrant waterfront district.

cinematic/028.description: The Fairmont Le Château Frontenac, formerly and commonly referred to as the Château Frontenac, is a historic hotel located in Quebec City, Quebec, Canada. The Château Frontenac was completed in 1893, and was designed by American architect Bruce Price. The hotel was a part of a series of Chateauesque-style hotels built for the Canadian Pacific Railway company during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

cool/014.title: File:Dülmen, Münsterstraße, Viktorkirmes, Riesenrad -- 2023 -- 9053 (kreativ 2).jpg
cool/014.description: Photo art based on a photo of the Viktorkirmes (Ferris wheel “Columbia Rad” on Münsterstraße, corner Coesfelder Straße) in Dülmen, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe original shot used the creative technique of intentional camera movement. This image was mirrored and recomposed several times to achieve the symmetry effect.Two traditional folk festivals are particularly important in Dülmen: the Trinity Fair and the Victor Fair.The Trinity Fair takes place around the church festival of the Holy Trinity in early summer. Its origins lie in religious celebrations and processions, which over time developed into a secular folk festival. With market stalls, fairground rides, and music, it became an integral part of urban life.The Viktorkirmes in autumn is associated with the patron saint of the city, Saint Victor. It also has ecclesiastical roots and dates back to earlier church consecration and patron saint festivals. In the 19th and 20th centuries, both fairs grew into important annual markets.Today, modern rides, traditional stalls, and a colorful supporting program characterize both festivals. They combine religious tradition, customs, and communal celebration and are an integral part of Dülmen’s identity.

cool/015.title: File:Visby, Gotland, Sweden (6126619340).jpg
cool/015.description: Visby – a modern world heritage site Visby is a modern county town with a medieval core. Here, innovative design, cutting-edge technology and the old ring wall, now over 700 years old, live comfortably side by side. Here there’s partying galore, great shopping and a network of rose-clad lanes. The city that now rises out of the sea to greet us is indeed one of many faces. The ring wall, the townscape and its many historical buildings gave the medieval Hanseatic city of Visby a place on UNESCO’s world heritage list in. The ring wall, almost 3.5 kilometres in length, is one of the world’s best preserved city walls. Several of the magnificent medieval trading houses, with their characteristic stepped gables, are still standing, as are many of the 17th and 18th-century wooden buildings. The Historical Museum gives fascinating insights into the history of the whole of Gotland. Can’t wait to find out more about historical Gotland? Read more here! A stroll through cobbled lanes, past the ruins and the Cathedral and across Klinten, a popular spot from which to take in the view of the town silhouetted against the sea, is a great opportunity to brush up on your cultural history and pop into one of the shops or enjoy a cup of coffee in one of the town’s many cafés. In Visby, nothing is very far away. Visby has several good shopping districts. Wisbyströvet, offering a huge range of clothes and shoes, cafés a-plenty and lots more, runs from Östertorg through Österport, into the central city via Hästgatan and then continues down Adelsgatan to Södertorg. In the St. Hans district you’ll find shops selling furnishing, clothes, delicatessen and a whole lot more besides. In the district between Almedalen and the port, modern Visby meets up with its medieval counterpart. This is the site of the university, accommodated in an old malt factory, the Almedal library, which opened in 2001, and, as of spring 2007, the Wisby Strand Congress Hall. The library and congress hall are excellent examples of how architecture, technology and the natural world can come together in a near ideal environment. In both buildings, the waters of the Baltic and the rays of the sun are used to keep the interior cool in summer and warm in the winter. In other words, a comfortable indoor climate that has a minimum impact on the environment. The Botanical Garden, planted in 1856, is a must for gardening enthusiasts. Throughout the year, visitors can enjoy exotic trees and plants alongside common Swedish species. Southern trees include magnolia, almond, mulberry, acacia and sweet chestnut. Read more about “Green Gotland”! As the long summer evening settles softly over the city, partygoers and eaters-out hit the streets. Today, Visby boasts more restaurants per capita than any other town in Sweden, all year round. Gotland cuisine almost always makes use of locally sourced products, such as asparagus, lamb and fish and even a few local exotics like ramsons and sand leeks. Read more about food and drink on Gotland. The partying district runs from Stora Torget along Strandgatan to the port. On summer nights, merrymakers throng the streets in almost continental fashion as they head towards the many restaurants. It’s an atmosphere in which culture thrives. You can see that here, artists, musicians and poets are very much at home.

cool/023.title: File:Münster, LVM -- 2017 -- 9343-7.jpg
cool/023.description: Office buildings of the LVM, Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe origins of today’s LVM campus date back to 1967, when the company moved into its premises on Kolde-Ring at the corner of Weseler Straße. The campus’s seven insurance buildings are located south of Münster city center. The northern part of Von-Stauffenberg-Straße runs through the campus, while Sperlichstraße forms its southern boundary. The LVM buildings were constructed between 1965 and 2014. The insurance company’s three high-rise buildings in particular are part of Münster’s skyline. The ground-level sculptures “Body and Soul” (Duk-Kyu Ryang, 2015) and “Two Horses for Münster” (Stephan Huber, 2002) are central visual elements of the site design. The 63-meter-high office tower LVM 5, known as “Crystal”, is a particularly striking building due to its design, while the 80-meter-high building LVM 3, known as “Tower”, is one of the tallest buildings in Münster.

cool/025.title: File:Dawn from the Fortezza, Orvieto.jpg
cool/025.description: Orvieto was already glowing when I settled onto a rampart of Fortezza Albornoz. A ridge of the Apennines held the sunrise for a few heartbeats, then light spilled over the range, bathing the fortress wall in gold and stirring the valley below to life. From this perch you can glimpse the city’s modern edge - highway, rail line, and the Paglia River - yet inside these ancient walls everything feels hushed and timeless. The day before had been pure Umbrian pleasure: weaving through medieval lanes, studying classical art, lingering over a pizza lunch with a chilled glass of Orvieto Classico, then trading stories over local reds and a final splash of amaro with a growing circle of new friends. Morning brought its own gentle ritual. We swung the hotel windows wide, letting bells and birdsong ride the cool breeze straight inside. I laced up for a jog but soon drifted into easy wandering. Historic facades, sculptures, and cliff-edge vistas blend art and nature into a single, seamless spectacle. It felt like a saturation point of beauty, the kind that overflows the senses and leaves room only to look up in quiet praise to the Lord. Later we stepped into a thousand-year-old church perched on the opposite wall of the city - another moment of threefold splendor: art-filled interiors, distinctive architecture from an earlier age, and wide natural vistas of Umbrian countryside where stone cottages and cypress spires dotted the rolling green like a living postcard. This photograph, one of many from my time there, tries to hold on to those layered moments: ancient stones, new friendships, and a sunrise that filled the soul.

cool/027.title: File:Nepal - Sagamartha Trek - 140 - The odd landscape crossing the Ngozumpa Glacier (497688171).jpg
cool/027.description: This was the strangest glacier landscape I've ever seen. Covered in rocky ridges, hills and gullies, lakes and streams, even sand dunes (left) and plants, the only visible part of the actual glacier were these ice cliffs ringing this semi-frozen lake. It was a very cool landscape, but would also be an easy one to get lost in if the cloud layer had dropped any lower. The Ngozumpa Glacier is the longest in Nepal. We had to cross the glacier while going from Gokyo to Dragnag (Thangnak) enroute to the Cho La.

moody/001.title: File:Interior of the library at Amantaka luxury Resort & Hotel in Luang Prabang Laos.jpg
moody/001.description: Interior of the library at Amantaka luxury resort and hotel in Luang Prabang, Laos. Amantaka is one of the 34 properties operated by Aman Resorts, hospitality company founded by Indonesian hotelier Adrian Zecha in 1988, present in more than 20 countries. The hotel, located in a French colonial building, opened in September 2009. A boutique hotel, it contains 24 lavish suites, encircling a central courtyard and is designed with a mixture of traditional Lao furnishings and modern. The suites range in size from 70 to 120 square metres and include eight suites, four pool suites, eight Khan pool suites, two Mekong pool suites and two Amantaka pool suites. All of the rooms have a king-sized bed centre of the suites beneath a traditional high ceiling. They are painted in white and have mahogany or dark wood furnishings and windows. The rooms are reported to be between 1,100 USD and 1,900 USD a night.

moody/003.title: File:Borkum, Hauptstrand -- 2020 -- 2691 (bw).jpg
moody/003.description: Beach chairs on the main beach, Borkum, Lower Saxony, GermanyThe beaches of Borkum are closely linked to the island’s development as a seaside resort and health spa. Originally, they were natural dune and sand landscapes that were constantly changing due to wind and tides.In the 19th century, the emergence of seaside resorts brought about a change. In 1834, Borkum was officially recognized as a North Sea resort. During this period, the first bathing facilities, beach chairs, and separate bathing areas for men and women were created. To protect the island and its beaches from storm surges, dunes were reinforced in the 19th century and later groynes and revetments were built. These coastal protection measures shape the appearance of the beaches today.In the 20th century, Borkum developed into a popular holiday destination. The north beach became the main bathing beach, while the extensive south beach is considered a nature and relaxation area.

moody/014.title: File:Reception lounge at Amantaka luxury Resort & Hotel at blue hour in Luang Prabang Laos.jpg
moody/014.description: Reception lounge at Amantaka luxury resort and hotel at blue hour in Luang Prabang, Laos, with couch and a candle lantern on the low table. Amantaka is one of the 34 properties operated by Aman Resorts, hospitality company founded by Indonesian hotelier Adrian Zecha in 1988, present in more than 20 countries. The hotel, located in a French colonial building, opened in September 2009. A boutique hotel, it contains 24 lavish suites, encircling a central courtyard and is designed with a mixture of traditional Lao furnishings and modern. The suites range in size from 70 to 120 square metres and include eight suites, four pool suites, eight Khan pool suites, two Mekong pool suites and two Amantaka pool suites. All of the rooms have a king-sized bed centre of the suites beneath a traditional high ceiling. They are painted in white and have mahogany or dark wood furnishings and windows. The rooms are reported to be between 1,100 USD and 1,900 USD a night.

moody/024.title: File:Münster, WestLotto -- 2013 -- 3296.jpg
moody/024.description: Office building near the entrance of the Westdeutsche Lotterie (at the Weseler Straße), Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe WestLotto headquarters in Münster is the main office of Westdeutsche Lotterie (WestLotto). An architectural competition was held in 1958 to design the headquarters, which was won by renowned Münster architect Harald Deilmann. The modern company building was constructed on a plot of land on Weseler Straße that was still undeveloped at the time and made a clear architectural statement for the development of a new service district in the city. Since its original construction, the building has been expanded and renovated several times.For the central building, Harald Deilmann used a clearly structured grid that is both urban and functional and is reflected in both the façade and the interior design. The façade combines black glazed clinker bricks with aluminum windows, giving the building a distinctive appearance in the urban context despite its functionality.In 1978, the building was given a striking black tower, which further developed the architectural design language. In 1994, the administration building was raised, contributing to a change in its silhouette.

moody/026.title: File:Slush on window in three lights.jpg
moody/026.description: Slush deposited on the lower part of a window pane during snowing mixed with rain in Gåseberg, Lysekil Municipality, Sweden. The slush was photographed as it accumulated, first in daylight, then in blue dusk and last at night illuminated from the outside by a sodium street light and a halogen floodlight, giving a mixed light source. The camera position is not exactly the same in each frame, since I just took the photos when I saw the slush growing on the window. The idea of doing a triptych came much later when I was processing the photos. The size of the photos are scaled to match the size of the "slush particles" since they were taken at different distances from the window. The relative height of the slush deposit is correct. As the slush hit the window, it slid downwards (causing some motion blur) and became more compacted.

moody/029.title: File:Münster, Freiherr-vom-Stein-Haus -- 2021 -- 9042-6.jpg
moody/029.description: Freiherr-vom-Stein-Haus (district government) on Domplatz in Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Freiherr vom Stein House on Domplatz in Münster was built between 1753 and 1757 as a Baroque aristocratic palace for Johann Heinrich von Droste zu Vischering. The impressive sandstone façade and spacious interior layout reflect the influence of the Westphalian aristocracy in the 18th century.The house is named after Freiherr vom Stein, who resided here in 1816/17 as president of the newly created Prussian province of Westphalia. During this time, he implemented significant administrative reforms that modernized Prussia.Today, the Münster district government is the state authority for the Münsterland region. It coordinates tasks such as school supervision, local government supervision, environmental and regional planning. The historic building continues to serve as the administrative headquarters, combining Baroque architecture with modern state administration.

pastel/010.title: File:Blue Liberdade Hotel — Lisbon, Portugal (54868748695).jpg
pastel/010.description: The Blue Liberdade Hotel in Lisbon, Portugal, stands proudly along the elegant Avenida da Liberdade [EDIT: Praça dos Restauradores], where 19th-century grandeur meets modern sophistication. Its striking façade of cobalt-blue azulejos—Portugal’s signature glazed ceramic tiles—makes the building a luminous presence amid the white-stone and pastel façades of central Lisbon. The rhythmic repetition of tall French windows with wrought-iron balconies, crowned by a red-tiled mansard roof, reflects the city’s unique fusion of Parisian and Portuguese architectural influences. Originally part of a residential block built during Lisbon’s late-19th-century urban expansion, the structure was restored and adapted into a boutique hotel that blends tradition with contemporary comfort. The blue tiles shimmer differently throughout the day, catching Lisbon’s famous Atlantic light—from soft morning silver to the deep ultramarine tones of dusk. This dynamic play of color against the hotel’s crisp white trim and black balcony railings gives the building a timeless yet fresh character. Inside, the Blue Liberdade embraces understated luxury, offering a tranquil refuge in the heart of the capital. Its interiors carry echoes of the street’s architectural rhythm—clean lines, polished wood, and a palette of blue and neutral tones that nod to the façade outside. Guests can step directly from its doors into the pulse of Lisbon: the grand boulevard of Avenida da Liberdade lined with jacaranda trees, designer boutiques, and the soft sounds of fado spilling from nearby cafés. The hotel also sits just steps away from Restauradores Square, one of Lisbon’s most recognizable plazas, and within view of the Elevador da Glória, which climbs toward the Bairro Alto. The nearby Pinoquio restaurant, visible at street level in this photograph, is a beloved local spot known for its seafood and convivial outdoor seating—one of many small details that anchor the Blue Liberdade within Lisbon’s everyday life rather than apart from it. As the city continues to evolve, the Blue Liberdade Hotel embodies Lisbon’s commitment to honoring its architectural past while embracing its cosmopolitan future. It’s not simply a building painted blue—it’s a reflection of Lisbon’s soul: colorful, layered, resilient, and endlessly welcoming.

pastel/021.title: File:Bonn, Sterntor -- 2020 -- 6505.jpg
pastel/021.description: Sterntor in Bonn, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Sterntor is the last remaining city gate of Bonn’s medieval city fortifications. Its origins date back to the 13th century, when Bonn was surrounded by a city wall with several gates. The Sterntor formed the northern entrance to the city and was part of the defensive fortifications of the Electorate of Cologne.Over the centuries, the gate lost its military significance. When large parts of the city wall were demolished in the 19th century, the Sterntor was initially preserved, but was demolished in 1898 due to road construction measures.On the initiative of Bonn citizens, the structure was rebuilt in 1900 at its current location—close to its original site—using historical components. Stones from other demolished city gates were also integrated into the new structure.

pastel/025.title: File:First Light Through Palms (54415533856).jpg
pastel/025.description: This was taken at the Fontainebleau in Miami Beach, on the last morning of a work trip that doubled as spring break. The day before, I’d gone out early to the beach hoping to catch sunrise with some palm trees in the frame, but the composition just wasn’t there—too open, no good angles. So later that day, during a break between sessions, I started keeping an eye out for a better spot. I came across a rooftop with an external staircase—nothing too dramatic, but it gave me just enough height to see over the hedges and line up the palms with the ocean and sky. When I returned the next morning, the view was exactly what I’d hoped for. The sun was just starting to break the horizon, sitting perfectly between the trees. The colors were more varied than I expected—still some cool blue in the sky, blending into soft oranges, a hint of red, and that gold you only get right at sunrise. The palms added just enough structure to the frame without stealing attention from the light. It was quiet, still, and kind of a perfect little moment before the workday picked up again.

pastel/026.title: File:DFC 3893 Gentle waves wash over rocky tidepools as pastel light from the setting sun paints the calm sea and shoreline.jpg
pastel/026.description: This is a serene coastal landscape captured during what appears to be either sunset or sunrise. The photo shows a tranquil seascape with dark rocks scattered across the shoreline. The water has a smooth, misty appearance, created by using a long exposure technique in photography that gives the waves a dreamy, silky texture as they flow around the rocks. The horizon stretches across the frame with soft pastel colors in the sky - gentle pinks and oranges blending with blue tones. The wet sand on the beach reflects the light from the sky, creating a mirror-like effect. The overall mood is peaceful and contemplative, showcasing the natural beauty of the coast during the golden hour. The composition balances the static elements of the rocks against the flowing movement of the water, creating a sense of timelessness.

portrait/001.title: File:Environmental Portrait of Stephanie Fernandez (jsc2020e041336).jpg
portrait/001.description: jsc2020e041336 (Sep 21, 2020) - “There are obvious highlights [about my job] and then there are some highlights that I didn’t really see coming. It’s such an honor to be just one person behind the scenes supporting the crewmembers while they’re up in space and focusing on their missions. It’s such an honor to be a part of them accomplishing their mission. The first time I got a call from space was a major highlight. Every call I’ve gotten from space since then has never gotten less exciting. The fact that I am continuously communicating with people who are living and working on the International Space Station — it’s just very surreal, it’s very mind-blowing still to this day even though I’ve been in this role for several years now. "But one of the highlights that I didn’t necessarily see coming is that a lot of the crewmembers have become mentors to me now. I look up to them for so many different reasons. And we have such a diverse corps that there’s really unlimited wisdom that I can take from listening to each crewmember’s story and from working with each crewmember. They show so much humility and perseverance. Hearing stories about their failures and their successes, watching them problem-solve and even problem-solving with them, has made me grow to be the kind of individual that I think I always wanted to become. I just never knew that I would have the opportunity to have these kinds of mentors to actually help me become that person.” — Stephanie Fernandez, Social Media and Outreach Specialist for the Astronaut Office, Johnson Space Center Image Credit: NASA / James Blair Interviewer: NASA / Thalia Patrinos
portrait/001.creator: NASA Johnson Space Center / James Blair - NASA - JSC
portrait/001.credit: This image or video was catalogued by Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center of the United States National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) under Photo ID: jsc2020e041336.

portrait/013.title: File:NIOSH Aerosol Sampler (8743397933).jpg
portrait/013.description: This picture shows the NIOSH aerosol sampler as it was worn by healthcare workers during a study of airborne influenza. The inlet of the sampler is close to the mouth and nose of the worker, in what is called the worker’s “breathing zone”. This ensures that the sampler is collecting aerosol particles representative of those that the worker is breathing. The sampler is attached to the strap of a backpack that contains a vacuum pump to pull air through the sampler.

portrait/026.description: Elizabeth Mukwimba is a 62-year-old Tanzanian smallholder farmer who now has solar lighting and a cleaner cookstove in her home, thanks to schemes backed by UK aid. Elizabeth has had an M-Power solar panel and lights fitted in her home by Off Grid Electric, a private sector company dedicated to providing sustainable, affordable energy to people in developing countries who aren't connected to the electricity grid. It means that Elizabeth now has lighting at home at night, which means she doesn't have to buy expensive kerosene. The money she's saved already has helped her put a new tin roof on her house. It also means her grandchildren can read and do their homework in the evening. She also has a 'clean cookstove', as seen in this picture. These cookstoves provide a safer, more durable and energy efficient means of cooking as opposed to traditional open fire cooking. The ceramic, metal-encased design means that much smaller amounts of wood or charcoal are needed, thus reducing the amount of time and money people need to spend on procuring fuel. The design is therefore also much healthier, producing much less smoke or carbon monoxide. "I feel much better because of the solar lighting and the cookstove", says Elizabeth. "Before, I sometimes had to choose between buying vegetables to eat, or buying kerosene to light the lamps with at night. Sometimes we used to have to rely just on matches for lighting in the dark. "But now I'm not having to buy kerosene and the cookstove uses much less fuel, so I'm saving money and not worrying so much about how to provide for my family. It means I can start to save money to hopefully build a new house in the future". In less than 2 years, Off Grid Electric has installed solar power systems in over 22,000 homes across Tanzania, meaning many more people now have access to cheap, renewable electricity - a vital step forward in a country where less than 14% of the population are connected to the electricity grid. The Tanzania Improved Cook Stoves programme, implemented by the Dutch NGO SNV, aims to provide improved cooking facilities for 45,000 people in Tanzania's Lake Zone region by 2017. 28,500 people have already benefitted. Picture: Russell Watkins/Department for International Development.

street/001.title: File:Münster, LVM-Versicherung -- 2014 -- 3276.jpg
street/001.description: Crosswalk between the two towers of the LVM insurance in Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe origins of today’s LVM campus date back to 1967, when the company moved into its premises on Kolde-Ring at the corner of Weseler Straße. The campus’s seven insurance buildings are located south of Münster city center. The northern part of Von-Stauffenberg-Straße runs through the campus, while Sperlichstraße forms its southern boundary. The LVM buildings were constructed between 1965 and 2014. The insurance company’s three high-rise buildings in particular are part of Münster’s skyline. The ground-level sculptures “Body and Soul” (Duk-Kyu Ryang, 2015) and “Two Horses for Münster” (Stephan Huber, 2002) are central visual elements of the site design. The 63-meter-high office tower LVM 5, known as “Crystal”, is a particularly striking building due to its design, while the 80-meter-high building LVM 3, known as “Tower”, is one of the tallest buildings in Münster.

street/010.title: File:Perth (AU), Elizabeth Quay Bridge -- 2019 -- 0375-9.jpg
street/010.description: Elizabeth Quay Bridge, Perth, Western Australia, AustraliaThe Elizabeth Quay Bridge is a striking pedestrian bridge in Perth city center. It was built as part of the Elizabeth Quay urban development project, which aimed to strengthen the connection between the city center and the Swan River. The bridge opened in 2016.Its curved, asymmetrical design is characteristic. Two sloping steel arches support the structure and give it a dynamic, modern shape. With a length of around 110 meters, it spans a side arm of the Swan River and connects the promenade with the island in the harbour basin.The bridge is intended exclusively for pedestrians and cyclists and offers views of the Perth skyline. In the evening, it is illuminated and is a popular photo motif. The Elizabeth Quay Bridge symbolizes the urban renewal of the waterfront area and the opening of the city to the water.

street/019.title: File:Duisburg, Landschaftspark Duisburg-Nord, Hochofenstraße -- 2025 -- 7441 (cinematic).jpg
street/019.description: Blast furnace road (at night) Landschaftspark Duisburg-Nord, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe light installation was put into operation as permanent lighting in December 1996. It was designed by British artist Jonathan Park. The three colors red, green and blue are reminiscent of the functions of the ironworks: fire and heat, gas and water.The Landschaftspark Duisburg-Nord (also known as “LaPaNo” or “LaPaDu” for short) is a landscape park covering around 180 hectares surrounding a disused steelworks in Duisburg-Meiderich. The former plant was founded in 1902 by August Thyssen as a “joint-stock company for metallurgical operations” to supply his Siemens-Martin steelworks in Bruckhausen and Styrum, and went into operation on May 16, 1903, with the firing up of blast furnace 1. In 1985, blast furnace 5 was shut down after just twelve years of operation due to overcapacity on the European steel market, making it the last remaining blast furnace. In the 1990s, the halls, buildings, and outdoor areas were redesigned according to plans by architects Peter Latz + Partner. In 1994, the park was opened to the public.

street/020.title: File:Sydney (AU), Darling Harbour -- 2019 -- 3199-201 (cinematic).jpg
street/020.description: Darling Harbour, Sydney, New South Wales, AustraliaDarling Harbour in Sydney is located on the eastern edge of the city center and has a long and varied history. Originally, the area was a natural bay and river connection used by the Gadigal and Eora communities. In the 19th century, the harbour developed into an important hub for goods, shipping, and naval activities, with customs facilities, shipyards, and warehouses dominating the landscape. Through land reclamation and redevelopment, Darling Harbour gradually transformed from a working port to a leisure, recreation, and exhibition district.In the 1980s, a strategic shift took place: urban redevelopment, new infrastructure, and major events led to increased use as an urban leisure and MICE location. With the Bicentennial in 1988, the area was redesigned, and museums, convention centers, restaurants, and green spaces were created. Today, Darling Harbour combines history with science, culture, and leisure and remains a vibrant attraction in the heart of Sydney.

street/025.title: File:Münster, Torminbrücke -- 2015 -- 7447.jpg
street/025.description: Tormin Bridge (at sunrise), Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe 145-meter-long Tormin Bridge is a pedestrian and bicycle bridge in Münster. It spans the Aasee lake and connects parts of the city center with adjacent residential and green areas.It was built in the 20th century as part of urban transport and road planning. The aim was to create safe crossings for pedestrians and, in particular, for cyclists, who are very numerous in Münster. The bridge has a functional design and blends inconspicuously into the cityscape.The design is by architects Fritz König and Albert Thomas. It was named after Richard Tormin, an engineer and city planning officer. He was also responsible for the previous structure, built in 1926. The current bridge was inaugurated on May 27, 1989.

street/027.description: Restaurants and pier at the Aasee, Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Aasee is an artificial lake southwest of Münster city center. Its origins date back to the early 20th century. Between 1914 and 1926, the Aa river was dammed to regulate flooding and create a local recreation area. In the 1970s, the lake was expanded and redesigned.The Aasee is around 2.3 kilometers long and surrounded by spacious green areas. Walking and cycling paths, sailing and pedal boat facilities, and sunbathing lawns make it a popular meeting place. Cultural institutions such as the LWL Museum of Art and Culture and the Allwetterzoo are also nearby.Today, the Aasee combines flood protection, leisure, and culture. It is considered the “green heart” of Münster and shapes the cityscape as well as the lifestyle of this bicycle-friendly university town.

street/029.title: File:Hamburg, Speicherstadt, Block P -- 2016 -- 3330-6.jpg
street/029.description: Block P (and Wandbereiterbrücke) in the Speicherstadt, Hamburg at night, GermanyThe Speicherstadt was built between 1885 and 1927 as the world’s largest contiguous warehouse complex on over 100,000 oak and poplar piles to store goods duty-free, which became necessary when Hamburg was integrated into the German customs territory in 1888. Its special feature is the unique combination of red brick buildings in neo-Gothic style, waterways (fleets), bridges, and streets, which, together with the Kontorhaus district, make it a maritime UNESCO World Heritage Site. Noteworthy buildings include the iconic moated castle and museums such as the Speicherstadtmuseum, the Coffee Museum, the Spice Museum, and the German Customs Museum.

travel/004.title: File:Gütenbach, Teichschlucht -- 2025 -- 7668.jpg
travel/004.description: Teichbach Waterfall in Teichbach Valley near Gütenbach/Simonswald, Baden-Württemberg, GermanyThe Teichschlucht is a wildly romantic gorge in the southern Black Forest. A narrow stream has carved its way deep into the rock here, creating a narrow, rocky landscape with steep slopes, waterfalls, and moss-covered rocks. Narrow paths and footbridges lead through the valley, making the gorge an impressive natural experience.The adjacent protected forest is a strictly protected forest area. In a protected forest, humans no longer interfere with natural development. Old trees are allowed to die and remain as deadwood, creating a pristine, structurally rich forest with high biodiversity. Mosses, fungi, insects, and rare bird species find valuable habitat here.The pond gorge and protected forest exemplify the wild, natural side of the Black Forest and stand for consistent nature and species conservation.

travel/006.title: File:Münster, Prinzipalmarkt -- 2014 -- 4516-20-2.jpg
travel/006.description: Prinzipalmarkt, Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyPrinzipalmarkt is a street in Münster’s old town. The name means “main market”, in contrast to the nearby streets Roggenmarkt and Fischmarkt. With its layout and buildings, Prinzipalmarkt documents the historical and architectural development of Münster’s economic and political center. Its development as a bourgeois market street on the edge of the cathedral precinct and on the eastern border of the cathedral began in the 12th century. A closed development probably existed around 1280. At the turn of the 13th to 14th century, the arcade that characterizes the Prinzipalmarkt was built. It was not until the beginning of the 17th century that the name Prinzipalmarkt came into use. During World War II, most of the buildings on Prinzipalmarkt were completely destroyed, some down to their cellars and ground floors. Thanks to reconstruction work carried out between 1947 and 1958, Prinzipalmarkt was able to retain its character as an ensemble in the form of a historic market square. Compared to its pre-war state, many buildings were significantly altered, but the original impression remained intact.

travel/008.title: File:Dülmen, Alte Brennerei Löhning -- 2015 -- 8609-13.jpg
travel/008.description: Interior (apartment) of the old distillery Löhning, Dülmen, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Löhning distillery was a traditional grain distillery in Dülmen and is a prime example of the long distilling tradition in the Münsterland region.Its origins date back to the 19th century, when many farms began processing surplus grain into grain brandy. The Löhning family also took advantage of this opportunity and developed an independent distillery from a secondary agricultural business. The main product was grain brandy – typical for Westphalia – made from regional rye or wheat.Over the decades, the company modernized its technology, but remained artisanal in character. Like many smaller distilleries, Löhning had to compete with large industrial producers in the 20th century. Thanks to its regional roots, consistent quality, and traditional recipes, the distillery was able to maintain its position in the local market.The Löhning distillery in Dülmen was closed in 1989. The building was then renovated in accordance with historic preservation guidelines and converted into a KfW efficiency house, among other things.

travel/011.description: Lake Minnewanka in Banff National Park, Alberta, CanadaBanff National Park in the Canadian province of Alberta was founded in 1885 and is Canada’s oldest national park. Its creation dates back to the discovery of hot springs on Sulphur Mountain, after which the area was placed under protection. Today, the park covers around 6,600 square kilometers in the Canadian Rocky Mountains and is part of the UNESCO World Natural Heritage Site “Canadian Rocky Mountain Parks”.The landscape is characterized by rugged peaks, glaciers, turquoise lakes such as Lake Louise, and dense coniferous forests. The park provides habitat for grizzly bears, wapiti, moose, and bighorn sheep. In addition to protecting the unique mountain environment, Banff is an important destination for hikers, skiers, and nature enthusiasts from all over the world.

travel/016.title: File:San Francisco (CA, USA), Alcatraz -- 2022 -- 3014 (cinematic).jpg
travel/016.description: View (from Fisherman’s Wharf) of Alcatraz Island at sunset, San Francisco, California, USAAlcatraz, the rocky island in San Francisco Bay, transformed from a strategic military base to the most notorious maximum security prison in the United States. Originally designated as a military fort to secure the gold rush town in 1850, it later housed the army’s first military prison. Due to its isolated location and treacherous, freezing currents, the Department of Justice took over the island in 1934 and turned it into a federal prison.Here, under the strictest regime, serious criminals such as Al Capone and “Birdman” Robert Stroud were interned. The concept was simple: anyone who broke the rules ended up in Alcatraz – a place without privileges. Despite 14 escape attempts, the prison is officially considered escape-proof, even if the spectacular escape of 1962 still leaves questions unanswered today. In 1963, operations were discontinued due to high maintenance costs. Another important chapter in the prison’s history is its occupation by activists from the “Indians of All Tribes” (1969–1971), who used the island as a symbol for indigenous rights. Today, Alcatraz is part of the National Recreation Area and attracts millions of visitors annually as a museum.

travel/018.title: File:Münster, Schlossplatz, Herbstsend, Kettenkarussell -- 2024 -- 6459.jpg
travel/018.description: Swing ride “Wellenflug” at the Herbstsend in Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Send is Münster’s big folk festival and takes place three times a year on Schlossplatz square. Its origins date back to the 9th century. The name is derived from the Middle Low German word “Synode”, meaning church assembly. In the Middle Ages, this synod was combined with a market, which over time developed into an annual fair.Today, the Send is one of the largest folk festivals in the Münsterland region. Fairground rides, stalls, music, and fireworks characterize the event and attract hundreds of thousands of visitors.A historical symbol is the Send sword at the historic town hall. This sword was formerly erected during the Send as a sign of market peace. It stood for protection, legal certainty, and special order during the market. Even today, the Send sword commemorates the medieval roots of the festival and combines tradition with lively folk culture.

Despite being almost completely rebuilt in the 16th century, the castle has largely retained its fortified character. It developed into the ancestral seat of the Droste zu Vischering family.

travel/028.title: File:Duisburg, Landschaftspark Duisburg-Nord, Erzbunker -- 2024 -- 4214.jpg
travel/028.description: Ore bunker in the Landschaftspark Duisburg-Nord, Duisburg, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Landschaftspark Duisburg-Nord (also known as “LaPaNo” or “LaPaDu” for short) is a landscape park covering around 180 hectares surrounding a disused steelworks in Duisburg-Meiderich. The former plant was founded in 1902 by August Thyssen as a “joint-stock company for metallurgical operations” to supply his Siemens-Martin steelworks in Bruckhausen and Styrum, and went into operation on May 16, 1903, with the firing up of blast furnace 1. In 1985, blast furnace 5 was shut down after just twelve years of operation due to overcapacity on the European steel market, making it the last remaining blast furnace. In the 1990s, the halls, buildings, and outdoor areas were redesigned according to plans by architects Peter Latz + Partner. In 1994, the park was opened to the public.

vintage/007.title: File:Münster, Prinzipalmarkt, Arkaden -- 2021 -- 8931-3.jpg
vintage/007.description: Alley between Stadtweinhaus and City Hall, Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyPrinzipalmarkt is a street in Münster’s old town. The name means “main market”, in contrast to the nearby streets Roggenmarkt and Fischmarkt. With its layout and buildings, Prinzipalmarkt documents the historical and architectural development of Münster’s economic and political center. Its development as a bourgeois market street on the edge of the cathedral precinct and on the eastern border of the cathedral began in the 12th century. A closed development probably existed around 1280. At the turn of the 13th to 14th century, the arcade that characterizes the Prinzipalmarkt was built. It was not until the beginning of the 17th century that the name Prinzipalmarkt came into use. During World War II, most of the buildings on Prinzipalmarkt were completely destroyed, some down to their cellars and ground floors. Thanks to reconstruction work carried out between 1947 and 1958, Prinzipalmarkt was able to retain its character as an ensemble in the form of a historic market square. Compared to its pre-war state, many buildings were significantly altered, but the original impression remained intact.

vintage/011.title: File:Dülmen, Rolls-Royce Phantom II -- 2024 -- 5825 (kreativ).jpg
vintage/011.description: Rolls-Royce Phantom II in Dülmen, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Rolls-Royce Phantom II was built by Rolls-Royce from 1929 to 1936 and was a further development of the Phantom I. It was considered the epitome of luxurious automotive engineering in the interwar period.Technically, the Phantom II had a 7.7-liter inline six-cylinder engine that was known for its smooth running and reliability. The chassis was improved, among other things, with a new frame construction and optimized suspension, which increased driving comfort and road holding.As was customary at the time, Rolls-Royce mainly supplied the chassis; the bodies were individually built by renowned coachbuilders such as Park Ward or Mulliner. This resulted in elegant limousines, Pullman models, and sporty variants such as the Continental.The Phantom II quickly became a status symbol for royalty, industrialists, and movie stars. It is one of the most sought-after pre-war classic cars and stands for technical perfection, quality craftsmanship, and timeless elegance.

vintage/013.title: File:Casio 3157 (8202948806).jpg
vintage/013.description: You know, aside from a brief childish interest at about age 12 when they were first introduced, I've always hated digital watches. I just like the whole art of mechanical watchmaking, and living and breathing computers all day, I don't really want to have one on my wrist, too. Still, when it came time to choose a watch for sailing, this Casio with a built in compass and thermometer, 200m water resistance, 5 alarms and multiple time zones, just couldn't be matched. It isn't one of their famous G-Shock watches, but I don't care. It'll almost certainly handle all the abuse I'm likely to dish out. Even its looks are growing on me (although I won't be throwing out any analog skeleton watches just yet). I admit, I occasionally permit myself dramatic and ridiculous dreams of being shipwrecked and in a life raft, needing the compass to navigate. Hopefully I won't get a chance to test how well either the watch or I perform in those situations, but if I ever do, well, it has a compass.

vintage/017.title: File:Münster, Send -- 2018 -- 1756 (cinematic).jpg
vintage/017.description: Chair-O-Plane at the Send, Münster, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe Send is Münster’s big folk festival and takes place three times a year on Schlossplatz square. Its origins date back to the 9th century. The name is derived from the Middle Low German word “Synode”, meaning church assembly. In the Middle Ages, this synod was combined with a market, which over time developed into an annual fair.Today, the Send is one of the largest folk festivals in the Münsterland region. Fairground rides, stalls, music, and fireworks characterize the event and attract hundreds of thousands of visitors.A historical symbol is the Send sword at the historic town hall. This sword was formerly erected during the Send as a sign of market peace. It stood for protection, legal certainty, and special order during the market. Even today, the Send sword commemorates the medieval roots of the festival and combines tradition with lively folk culture.

vintage/027.title: File:Krummhörn, Greetsiel, Hafen -- 2018 -- 1125.jpg
vintage/027.description: Ships at the harbour, Greetsiel, Krummhörn, Lower Saxony, GermanyThe fishing village of Greetsiel is located in Krummhörn on the North Sea coast of East Frisia. It was founded in the Middle Ages as a small port and was shaped by crab fishing and trade for centuries. The historic town center with its 17th-century gabled houses and the harbour with its fleet of crab boats still give Greetsiel its maritime character today.The Greetsiel twin mills at the entrance to the village are particularly well known. The two Dutch windmills date from the 19th century (1856 and 1921). They originally served as grain mills and were important components of regional agriculture. One of the mills burned down partially in 2019, but has been restored and rebuilt.Today, the windmills are landmarks of the village. They can be visited and provide insights into traditional milling technology. Together with the historic harbour, they characterize the typical image of Greetsiel and symbolize East Frisian coastal culture.

warm/001.title: File:Sunlit Chamber with Antique Charm in Sintra (54924590605).jpg
warm/001.description: Warm morning light spills through gauzy curtains, softening every detail of this intimate room in Sintra and giving it the feel of a lived-in memory. The space is arranged with a quiet sense of ceremony: a carved wooden bed dressed in deep red textiles, a round table set with porcelain and silver, and golden upholstered chairs that echo the glow of the window. The contrast between the cool blue walls and the vivid red carpet heightens the drama, creating a palette that feels both historic and unexpectedly vibrant. What stands out most is how the furniture carries the weight of its era—polished wood with confident curves, drawer pulls worn smooth from decades of use, and a lamp that still holds the shape of the hand that once lit it. Even the smallest objects seem to tell stories: a dish placed just so, a book left open as if the reader had stepped away only moments before. The room balances ornament and restraint. It invites curiosity without feeling staged, allowing the viewer to imagine lives once lived in its rhythm. Sunlight, texture, and craftsmanship come together to form a scene that lingers long after you step away.
warm/001.creator: Dale Cruse - 10M views from San Francisco, CA, USA
warm/001.credit: Sunlit Chamber with Antique Charm in Sintra

warm/017.title: File:People strolling across a bridge with a tram approaching, featuring colourful buildings and a crane in the background. (55246965929).jpg
warm/017.description: A vibrant street scene unfolds on a wide bridge with tram tracks running down the centre. A modern tram is approaching from the distance, its blue and yellow livery catching the sunlight. Several people are walking across the bridge in both directions, enjoying a sunny day. The backdrop is a picturesque cityscape of colourful, aged buildings with terracotta roofs, interspersed with greenery. A construction crane looms in the far background, suggesting ongoing development or renovation in the city. The bridge itself is robust, with a metallic structure and railings on either side, providing a clear walkway and tramway. The overall impression is one of a lively urban environment with a blend of historical architecture and modern transportation.
warm/017.creator: Michael Gaylard from Horsham, UK

warm/018.title: File:Harvested fields at sunset in Pomar de Valdivia, Palencia.jpg
warm/018.description: Aerial photograph taken with a drone showing the geometry of recently harvested agricultural fields in the municipality of Pomar de Valdivia, in the province of Palencia, autonomous community of Castile and León, Spain. The composition highlights the sinuous lines imprinted on the terrain by agricultural machinery (combine harvesters), straw bales stacked on the right, and the chromatic contrast between the golden straw in the foreground and the green plots in the background. The scene is illuminated by the warm, lateral light of the golden hour at sunset, outlining the relief of the limestone hills on the horizon.

wedding/028.title: File:Dülmen, Kirchspiel, St.-Jakobus-Kirche -- 2015 -- 5596.jpg
wedding/028.description: Wedding decorations in the St Jacob’s Church, Kirchspiel, Dülmen, North Rhine-Westphalia, GermanyThe church belongs to the former Marienburg Charterhouse – the only Carthusian monastery in Westphalia. The order came to Weddern in 1476/77 after the knight Gerd von Keppel donated his castle to the order. The charterhouse was inhabited until 1803.Construction of the church itself began in 1477, but remained unfinished for a long time; the keystone in the western bay bears the date 1607. After the monastery was dissolved, the building remained standing and is now used as a parish and community center.The church is a late Gothic place of worship built of red brick. The building has a deliberately simple design: a single-nave hall with a vaulted ceiling, choir area, and characteristic tracery windows. A tower with an octagonal pointed roof and dormers characterizes the exterior appearance. In 1871/72, the church was expanded in the neo-Gothic style, with a crypt for the von Croÿ family added and the interior expanded.Particularly noteworthy is the old choir stalls, which probably originate from Cologne and date from around 1350. Also impressive is a Rococo grille from 1752, which visually separates the altar area from the church and is artistically decorated.
